notes: WebEx had more than 5,000 customers at time of study publication April 2002
Virus-like word-of-mouth-induced growth rate of more than 800 new customers per quarter
40% of new customers referred by existing 5,000-customer installed base; word-of-mouth growth
WebEx built a private conferencing switched network on top of the Internet delivering QoS and security levels that vanilla Internet cannot match
Mediatone represents the third generation of WebEx switching infrastructure; provides multimedia switching capabilities

Level 1 of Aberdeen five-level model: face-to-face meetings offer best human interaction and full body language nuances; high travel cost and time are key drawbacks; trend clearly away from face-to-face especially for large internal meetings
Level 2: telephone calls are cheap and fast but connect only two parties; telephone tag is a business disease; limited to oral communication requiring supplementary fax or email
Level 3: audio conferencing connects many people at low cost but lacks visual nuances and requires coordination to share presentation materials
Level 4: video conferencing suits one-to-many broadcast or up to four conference rooms; high equipment costs; fixed locations; IP video plagued by firewall security forcing use of ISDN

Level 5: web conferences combine browser and audio connection (phone or VoIP); moderate costs; no capital investment beyond browser and internet; accessible anywhere; drawbacks are QoS and security over public Internet
WebEx emerged in the past year as the clear leader in high-end web conferencing with the greatest security, highest service quality, and dial-tone reliability
In 1999 WebEx was lumped with approximately 40 companies fighting to deliver shared PowerPoint presentations over the Internet
WebEx is more like a telephone utility than a software company; constructed worldwide business communications switching infrastructure
Post-9/11 travel uncertainty and 2001 recession shifted meetings away from mandatory face-to-face; first time socially acceptable to not conduct all meetings in person
Web conferencing is particularly attractive because it combines ubiquity and communication capability of PC-with-a-browser with familiarity and cost attractiveness of a conference call

Software-driven database-centric store-and-forward approach to online web conferencing by competitors led to many failures; only private network switched infrastructure can deliver highest security and service quality
UCF Universal Communications Format enables full PowerPoint animation support in Presentation Mode; previous DHTML attempts were unpredictable with significant limitations
UCF allows users to share Flash files embedded in PowerPoint presentations with start/pause/stop delivery control to all attendees
Access Anywhere enables secure access and sharing of information from unattended remote computer in a meeting; content stays secure while meeting participants can be anywhere
Mediatone multipoint video conferencing requires only a browser and simple webcam; also supports Polycam cameras and standard video camcorders
Mediatone Wireless PDA support enables participation in meetings with PDAs and use of Access Anywhere for remote information access
WebEx Training Center offers full training schedule, registration and approval management; shared control by simultaneous presenters panelists and trainees
Training Center includes questionnaires, test building and scoring, correction and grading; instant visual feedback to trainer verbal questions
Training Center supports SCORM industry standard for training data interchange
'Let's WebEx' — the noun becomes the verb; Aberdeen describes this as the ultimate compliment of market acceptance
Fundamental shift away from mandatory face-to-face meetings and toward web conferences described as tectonic; 2001 recession and post-9/11 travel uncertainty unleashed a market-shifting earthquake
WebEx goal is making online meetings as straightforward as — but much more productive than — telephone calls
Cisco Systems acquired WebEx Communications for $3.2 billion in 2007; brand continues as Cisco Webex
Zoom Video Communications founded 2011, launched 2013; grew rapidly with simpler UX and freemium model; dominated enterprise video conferencing by 2019-2020
COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 permanently accelerated web conferencing adoption; remote work became default for knowledge workers globally; the 'tectonic shift' prediction proved spectacularly accurate
Microsoft Teams launched 2017 became major competitor to Cisco Webex and Zoom; deeply integrated with Microsoft 365; now one of top three collaboration platforms globally
Adobe discontinued Flash Player December 31 2020; all major browsers removed support; UCF Flash sharing capability became obsolete; WebEx evolved its format support independently
PDA category effectively obsolete by ~2010; replaced by smartphones (iPhone launched 2007, Android 2008); WebEx mobile strategy shifted to smartphone apps
VoIP became the dominant telecommunications protocol globally; all major web conferencing platforms use VoIP exclusively by 2020s; traditional PSTN audio conferencing largely retired
ISDN decommissioned in most markets by early 2020s; video conferencing over IP became standard; the IP security concerns that drove ISDN use in 2002 were solved by enterprise VPN and encrypted SIP
Aberdeen Group acquired by Harte-Hanks in 2008; subsequently sold to Spiceworks Ziff Davis; brand continues publishing technology research under Spiceworks Ziff Davis umbrella
